Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-17#

Run ID: run-2026-03-17-ada490e9d8 · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-15 · Generated: 2026-03-17T09:37:52.050Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 19 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-16), government website accessibility scores have improved (+23.33 points, from 68 to 91.33). Performance scores have improved (+15.56 points, from 38 to 53.56).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 91.29 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

image-alt: Visual Content Access

Images without alternative text are completely invisible to blind users and to search engines. On government websites, informational images, diagrams, infographics, and document screenshots without alt text deprive blind citizens of the same information available to sighted visitors. Critical details like emergency instructions, benefit eligibility charts, or form procedure diagrams can be entirely inaccessible, putting these citizens at a disadvantage in accessing government services and information.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

html-has-lang: Language Identification

Without a language attribute, screen readers default to the user's system language settings, which may cause government content to be read in the wrong language with incorrect pronunciation. This is particularly harmful for multilingual citizens accessing government services in their non-primary language, and for users of braille displays that depend on language metadata for proper rendering.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with cognitive or language differences

aria-allowed-attr: Assistive Technology Reliability

Invalid ARIA attributes cause assistive technologies to misinterpret or malfunction on interactive controls. For screen reader users navigating complex government forms or applications, broken ARIA creates unpredictable behavior that can make tasks impossible to complete, denying access to essential services.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text assistive tools
  • People with motor disabilities using switch access or voice control

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

NN/g usability heuristics affected by today's top accessibility violations
# Heuristic Issue patterns URL violations Related axe rules
6 Recognition rather than recall 4 88 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, heading-order, link-name
4 Consistency and standards 4 79 landmark-one-main, heading-order, link-name, label-content-name-mismatch
8 Aesthetic and minimalist design 4 72 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block
10 Help and documentation 4 72 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block
1 Visibility of system status 3 58 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block
9 Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors 3 58 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block

Estimated Impact (daily)#

No accessibility findings data available for this scan. The impact estimation requires detailed accessibility findings from scanning tools. Currently, the scanner is running in a mode that does not collect individual accessibility issues.

Americans Excluded by Disability#

No accessibility findings data is available to estimate excluded users by disability category for this scan.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 99 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on March 17, 2026
Metric Estimated total (March 17, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 7 years, 8 months, 28 days 244,399,470 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~58.9 TB (2,449 copies of Wikipedia) Across 99 URLs with weight data

Extra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Extra data is calculated as: max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic.

https://fns.usda.gov 102786 poor 44 100 / 1 92 77
https://tsaenrollmentbyidemia.tsa.dhs.gov 102012 poor 46 85 / 2 50 92
https://marine.weather.gov 99234 poor 68 67 / 4 85 73
https://careers.cbp.gov 98341 poor 1 75 / 7 50 67
https://climate.gov 96294 poor 33 92 / 2 92 92
https://fsa.usda.gov 95090 poor 45 100 73 92
https://fbi.gov 94466 poor 45 79 / 3 77 54
https://fdrlibrary.org 94099 poor 48 79 / 4 81 77
https://kinetics.nist.gov 93283 good 99 74 / 4 73 100
https://randr19.nist.gov 93269 needs improvement 91 68 / 6 77 80
https://login.gov 91638 poor 66 100 100 92
https://about.usps.com 88867 poor 60 100 88 100
https://nhtsa.gov 88622 poor 52 85 / 5 54 100
https://epa.gov 86711 poor 53 100 96 100
https://army.mil 83536 unknown 0 0 0 0
https://www.usajobs.gov 81079 needs improvement 85 98 100 92
https://www.nist.gov 80832 poor 65 95 / 1 81 92
https://cancer.gov 80641 poor 74 97 / 2 100 92

Take Action#

Here is how you can help improve accessibility on U.S. government websites.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Element 1

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://forecast.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~29KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~29,559 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,955,876 affected page loads) ~8KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~8,868 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,955,876 affected page loads) ~138KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~138,926 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 2,955,876 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~29KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

https://weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Element 1

Element path: div.div-full > div#wwamap > div.USMap > img#us

Snippet:

<img id="us" src="https://www.weather.gov/wwamap/png/US.png" usemap="#US_large_imagemap_800_500">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 92

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~36KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~36,507 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,521,125 affected page loads) ~65KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~65,408 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,521,125 affected page loads)

Estimated ~15,211 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,521,125 affected page loads) ~4KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: html

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Accessibility Findings

https://usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 9

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~9,840 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,952 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads) ~21KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~21,648 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li.active

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="0" aria-checked="true" class="active">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="true"

Element 2

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="1" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 2

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element 3

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="2" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 3

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element 4

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="3" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Element 1

Element path: div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > nav > ul.nav-list

Snippet:

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has children which are not allowed: li[tabindex]
`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~9,840 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Affected elements (10):

Element 1

Element path: nav > ul.nav-list > li.qt-nav > a.nav-first-element

Snippet:

<a aria-expanded="false" role="menuitem" tabindex="0" aria-haspopup="true" class="nav-first-element menuitem" href="#">

Label: Quick Tools

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Required ARIA parents role not present: menu, menubar, group

Element path: nav > ul.nav-list > li.menuheader > a#mail-ship-width

  • Required ARIA parents role not present: menu, menubar, group

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~9,840 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,952 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: body > img

Snippet:

<img src="https://beacon.lynx.cognitivlabs.com/pixel/capture?tags=8dd8cf55-3ae9-49dc…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~23KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~23,616 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 983,989 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Element 1

Element path: li.global-footer--navigation-category > ol.global-footer--navigation-options > li > a

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~18KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~18,099 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 754,113 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,745 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 474,543 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,424 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 474,543 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~10,440 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 474,543 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,745 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 474,543 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,424 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 474,543 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: tbody > tr > td > table

Snippet:

<table cellspacing="1" cellpadding="2" border="0" width="100%">

Label: Hazard Tue (03/17) Wed (03/18) Thu (03/19) Fri (03/20) Sat (03/21) Sun (03/22) …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Accessibility Findings

https://studentaid.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 4

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~10KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~10,263 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 427,637 affected page loads) ~18KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~18,388 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 427,637 affected page loads)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Label: POPULAR TOPICS

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~10KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~10,263 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 427,637 affected page loads) ~18KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~18,388 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 427,637 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/announcements-events/idr-court-actions">

Label: visit StudentAid.gov/courtactions

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/fsa-id/sign-in/landing/?redirectTo=/my-aid">

Label: Log in

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~9,408 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 427,637 affected page loads) ~24KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Accessibility Findings

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,877 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 387,693 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,163 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 387,693 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

Snippet:

<button id="global-navigation-trigger--mobile" class="hds-global-menu-toggle menu-toggle menu-mobile-toggle" aria-expanded="false" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="left">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button.hds-search-panel-mobile-trigger

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element path: div#sbar > form#f > div > input

Snippet:

<input type="submit" value="Search">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.11 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #357ae8, font size: 10.0pt (13.3333px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~844 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 281,486 affected page loads) ~13KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~13,230 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 281,486 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~6,193 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 281,486 affected page loads) ~16KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Label: Notes

Accessibility Findings

https://coast.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Snippet:

<li class="menuitem" role="menuitem">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,498 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 249,768 affected page loads) ~749People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~749 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 249,768 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~5,994 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 249,768 affected page loads) ~10KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://itis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 3

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,055 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads) ~13KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~13,328 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: tr > td > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/citation.html">

Label: How to cite ITIS

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (67px by 14px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 14px instead of at least 24px.
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,298 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads) ~689People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~689 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,055 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html lang="en-US">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,298 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads) ~689People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~689 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 229,793 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: tbody > tr > td > table

Snippet:

<table style="text-align: left;" border="1" cellpadding="2" cellspacing="0" width="100%" summary="This nested table controls the structure of the page footer.">

Label: Monthly Export Scientific Names (any rank, any usage) Common Names 24-Feb-2026 …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,228 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 222,802 affected page loads) ~668People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~668 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 222,802 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

Snippet:

<button id="global-navigation-trigger--mobile" class="hds-global-menu-toggle menu-toggle menu-mobile-toggle" aria-expanded="false" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="left">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button.hds-search-panel-mobile-trigger

Snippet:

<button class="hds-search-panel-mobile-trigger search-mobile color-carbon-20 menu-toggle" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="right">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,210 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 220,977 affected page loads) ~663People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Label: Featured Topics

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://fas.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Accessibility Findings

https://ceac.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name)

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://egov.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 1

The document uses `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` (rule: meta-refresh)

Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience. Learn more about the refresh meta tag.

Estimated ~1,993 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 199,281 affected page loads) ~598People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~4,384 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 199,281 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://tsp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,678 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 167,835 affected page loads) ~504People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~504 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 167,835 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: aside#tsp-toolbar > ul > li > button#toggle-tsp-zoom

Snippet:

<button id="toggle-tsp-zoom" class="tooltip">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

https://eeoc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://earthquake.usgs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,338 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 133,805 affected page loads) ~401People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~401 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 133,805 affected page loads) ~6KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~6,289 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 133,805 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Affected elements (8):

Element 1

Element path: li > ul > li > a.external

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov" class="external" target="_blank">

Label: HHS.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size because it is partially obscured (smallest space is 59.4px by 19.2px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 15.8px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://history.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 3

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,283 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 128,282 affected page loads) ~385People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Element 1

Element path: div.region > div#block-fdic-theme-mainnavigation > nav#tbm-main > button.tbm-button

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,546 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 106,084 affected page loads) ~4KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~4,562 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 106,084 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.paragraph > div.mosaic-text-wrapper > div.field > div.field__item

Snippet:

<div class="field__item">

Label: BOARD MEETING

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.25 (foreground color: #38b6ff, background color: #ffffff, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element path: div.paragraph > div.mosaic-text-wrapper > div.field > div.field__item

`<frame>` or `<iframe>` elements do not have a title (rule: frame-title)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,061 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 106,084 affected page loads) ~318People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~318 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 106,084 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,334 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 106,084 affected page loads)

Element path: body.acquia-cms-toolbar > a.skipheader

Snippet:

Label: Skip to main content

Label: Here’s how you know

https://americanenglish.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Estimated ~1,059 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 105,936 affected page loads) ~318People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,542 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 105,936 affected page loads) ~4KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~4,555 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 105,936 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.fieldable-panels-pane > div.field-rta-html > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://americanenglish.state.gov/support-near-you-regional-english-langua…" target="_blank" rel="nofollow noopener">

Label: Regional English Language Officers (RELOs)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.98:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #1c2b58, surrounding text: #595a5c)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,448 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 102,012 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~992People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~992 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 99,234 affected page loads) ~298People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~298 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 99,234 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~4,664 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 99,234 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://careers.cbp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 75

Axe findings: 7

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div > div.ui-widget > c-crs-custom-modal > section.slds-modal

Snippet:

<section lwc-6m11k7ek9iv="" class="slds-modal slds-fade-in-open" role="dialog" tabindex="-1" aria-model="true" aria-labelledby="modal-heading-01-17">

Label: GET UP TO $60,000 IN INCENTIVES WHEN YOU JOIN CBP LET'S GO

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div > span.inline-text > span.inline-text > lightning-combobox.super-icon-override

Snippet:

<lightning-combobox lwc-60i0q72qjve="" class="super-icon-override slds-form-element" aria-label="Select Hiring Path" lwc-24on3e8rogg-host="" style="display: flex;">

Label: Select an Option

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a lightning-combobox with no valid role attribute.

Element 2

Element path: div.slds-align_absolute-center > div > span.inline-text > lightning-combobox.super-icon-override

Snippet:

<lightning-combobox lwc-60i0q72qjve="" class="super-icon-override slds-form-element" aria-label="Select Program Office" lwc-24on3e8rogg-host="" style="display: flex;">

Label: Select an Option

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a lightning-combobox with no valid role attribute.
`[aria-*]` attributes are not valid or misspelled (rule: aria-valid-attr)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid names. Learn more about valid ARIA attributes.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. `[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://www.nist.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~1,940 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 80,832 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~3,476 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 80,832 affected page loads)

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Accessibility Findings

https://cancer.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~1,774 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 80,641 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~4,677 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 80,641 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.usa-footer__address > div.usa-footer__contact-info > address > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov/">

Label: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (314.1px by 22.8px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21.6px instead of at least 24px.
